planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; Oldham's bamboo is a clumping species that does not spread invasively by underground rhizomes — each new cane emerges adjacent to the parent and the colony expands slowly outward, unlike the aggressively spreading running bamboos.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; Oldham's bamboo is one of the most important timber and edible bamboos for warm-climate gardens in zones 8b–12, producing straight, strong culms 3–4 inches in diameter and 30–50 feet tall that are used in construction, furniture, and crafts. Unlike the running (monopodial) bamboos that spread aggressively by underground rhizomes, this is a tight-clumping (sympodial) species that expands slowly and predictably without invasive spreading — making it safe to plant near structures and property boundaries. The large, young shoots in spring are edible — milder and less bitter than many bamboos. An excellent choice for tall tropical screening, erosion control on slopes, and sustainable bamboo production in the warm Southeast. Water generously in summer to support rapid growth of new culms.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring; when grown in the ground with ample water and nutrients, the internodes stay long and the plant grows tall and straight — the characteristic swollen 'buddha belly' internodes develop when the plant is stressed by container culture, drought, or root restriction. Grow in a large pot or restrict roots for the ornamental belly effect.

directions: Plant in fertile soil in full sun in zones 8–12; Buddha belly bamboo is a clumping tropical bamboo from southern China celebrated for its unique ability to produce dramatically swollen, compressed internodes when grown under stress — in restricted containers, drought conditions, or with root confinement. These short, bulging internodes resemble the rounded belly of a laughing Buddha statue, making individual culms highly ornamental and used in the crafting of Buddha belly canes sold in craft stores. In the open garden with adequate water and nutrients, the plant grows tall and straight with normal internodes; the 'belly' effect requires deliberate stress through container culture or root restriction. A non-invasive clumping bamboo that stays within its allotted space, making it safer for garden use than running species.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; IMPORTANT: Install a high-density polyethylene rhizome barrier (30 mil or thicker) at least 24–36 inches deep around all running bamboos before planting to prevent invasive spreading.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun, contained within a rhizome barrier; vivax bamboo is one of the tallest, fastest-growing temperate bamboos, with culms reaching 25–45 feet and diameters of 2–3.5 inches — making it among the most productive timber bamboos available in zones 5–10. The spring shoots are edible and moderately sweet; the culms are strong and used for construction, fencing, and crafts. Its extreme cold hardiness (to about –10°F / USDA zone 5) makes it one of the few large-statured bamboos reliable in cold climates. CAUTION: Like all Phyllostachys species, vivax is a spreading 'running' bamboo with invasive potential — rhizome barriers are mandatory in all garden settings. The cultivar 'Aureocaulis' has yellow culms with green stripes.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; install a 30-mil rhizome barrier 24–36 inches deep before planting. Sweetshoot bamboo produces the most palatable raw-edible shoots of any temperate bamboo.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun, contained with a rhizome barrier; sweetshoot bamboo earns its name from its exceptional culinary qualities — the spring shoots are notably less bitter than most bamboos and sweet enough to be eaten raw in thin slices, without the blanching required for most species. The shoots are harvested in spring as they emerge from the soil, before the sheaths open, when they are at peak sweetness and tenderness. Mature culms reach 15–25 feet and 1–2 inches in diameter, providing attractive screening material and lightweight bamboo poles. Like all Phyllostachys species, it spreads by underground rhizomes and requires containment. The coppery-bronze color of new culm sheaths in spring is ornamental. Plant in groves of 3 or more for heavier shoot production.

planting.note: Plant divisions of the rhizomatous colony in spring in moist to wet soil in partial shade to full sun; switchcane spreads by vigorous underground rhizomes to form dense thickets in appropriate moist conditions, mimicking its natural streambank and pocosin habitat.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun along streambanks, pond margins, and in wet bottomlands; switchcane is the smaller of two native North American bamboos (the other being giant cane, A. gigantea), naturally forming dense canebrake thickets in wet, poorly drained soils of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain. While historically common, natural canebrake habitats have been reduced by over 95% since European settlement, making switchcane increasingly rare in the wild. It provides important habitat for several rare and declining species, including the Swainson's warbler, which nests almost exclusively in canebrake thickets. Leaves are consumed by white-tailed deer and the cane structure provides winter cover for numerous birds. The Arogos skipper butterfly uses native cane as its larval host plant. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes — excellent for ecological restoration of wet areas, less suitable for small formal gardens.
